With the routine patches above—especially the corrected support-sandwich argument in the ordinary bidual followed by passage to the normal bidual—the proof establishes the full theorem for finite loopless simplicial graphs that are irreducible in the standard nontrivial-join sense and separable tracial vertex algebras each containing a trace-zero unitary.
solution_verified:
## Claim

Let \(\Gamma=(V,E)\) be a finite loopless simplicial graph with \(|V|\ge 3\). Assume \(\Gamma\) is irreducible in the standard nontrivial-join sense: there is no decomposition
\[
V=V_1\sqcup V_2,\qquad V_1,V_2\neq \varnothing,
\]
such that every vertex of \(V_1\) is adjacent to every vertex of \(V_2\). Let \((M_v,\tau_v)\), \(v\in V\), be separable tracial von Neumann algebras, and assume each \(M_v\) contains a unitary \(u_v\) with \(\tau_v(u_v)=0\). Let
\[
(M,\tau)=\star_\Gamma (M_v,\tau_v)
\]
be the tracial graph-product von Neumann algebra. Then \(M\) is properly proximal.

We prove this in the Boutonnet–Ioana–Peterson / DKEP von Neumann algebraic sense.

---

## 1. Notation and standard boundary formalism

For \(S\subset V\), write \(M_S\subset M\) for the parabolic graph-product subalgebra generated by \(\{M_s:s\in S\}\), with \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\). Let
\[
E_S:M\to M_S
\]
be the trace-preserving conditional expectation, and let
\[
e_S:L^2(M)\to L^2(M_S)
\]
be the Jones projection.

Let \(H=L^2(M)\). We write \(\lambda(x)\) for left multiplication by \(x\in M\), and
\[
\rho(x)=Jx^*J
\]
for right multiplication by \(x\). Thus \(\rho(x)\widehat y=\widehat{yx}\).

For \(S\subset V\), let \(X_S\subset B(H)\) be the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra generated by the operators
\[
\lambda(a)\rho(b)e_S,\qquad a,b\in M.
\]
Let \(K_S=K_{X_S}^{\infty,1}(M)\) be the DKEP \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closed compact ideal associated with \(X_S\). Define
\[
S_S(M)=\{T\in B(H):[T,\rho(x)]\in K_S\ \text{for every }x\in M\}.
\]
When \(X=K(H)\), we write \(S(M)\) for the absolute operator system.

We use the standard normal-bidual formulation of proper proximality. Namely, for every boundary piece \(X_S\), there is a normal-bidual operator system \(\widetilde S_S(M)\) containing the image of \(S_S(M)\), and a support projection \(q_S\) for the compact corner \(K_S\). The projection \(q_S\) commutes with both \(\lambda(M)\) and \(\rho(M)\), hence belongs to the absolute normal-bidual operator system \(\widetilde S(M)\). Moreover, if \(T\in \widetilde S_S(M)\), then
\[
[T,\rho(x)]\in q_S\mathcal A q_S
\]
inside the ambient normal-bidual \(C^*\)-algebra \(\mathcal A\). Thus, if \(r=1-q_S\), then
\[
[rTr,\rho(x)]=0
\]
for every \(x\in M\).

The normal-bidual criterion says that a separable tracial von Neumann algebra \(N\) is properly proximal iff there is no \(N\)-central state on \(\widetilde S(N)\) whose restriction to \(N\) is normal. Also, the support projections satisfy the following ordinary-bidual compatibility: if
\[
A=C^*(\lambda(M),\rho(M),e_{S_1},\dots,e_{S_n})\subset B(H)
\]
and \(B_S=A\cap X_S\) is the corresponding hereditary \(C^*\)-corner, with ordinary open support projection \(p_S\in A^{**}\), then in the normal bidual
\[
q_S=p_{\mathrm{nor}}p_S,
\]
where \(p_{\mathrm{nor}}\in A^{**}\) is the central projection defining the normal-bidual summand.

---

## 2. Elementary graph consequences

For \(v\in V\), write
\[
\operatorname{Link}(v)=\{w\in V\setminus\{v\}:(v,w)\in E\},
\]
\[
\operatorname{Star}(v)=\operatorname{Link}(v)\cup\{v\},
\]
and
\[
N(v)=V\setminus \operatorname{Star}(v).
\]

Since \(\Gamma\) is not a nontrivial join, \(N(v)\neq\varnothing\) for every \(v\in V\). Indeed, if \(\operatorname{Star}(v)=V\), then
\[
V=\{v\}\sqcup (V\setminus\{v\})
\]
would be a nontrivial join decomposition.

We shall also use the following consequence. If \(N(v)=\{w\}\), then there exists \(z\in \operatorname{Link}(v)\) such that \(z\) is not adjacent to \(w\). Otherwise \(w\) would be adjacent to every vertex of \(\operatorname{Link}(v)\), and then
\[
V=\{v,w\}\sqcup \operatorname{Link}(v)
\]
would be a nontrivial join decomposition, contradiction.

---

## 3. No central state on a link-boundary system

Fix \(v\in V\). Put
\[
L=\operatorname{Link}(v),\qquad B=M_L,
\]
\[
A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)},\qquad C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}}.
\]
The graph-product decomposition gives
\[
M=A*_{B}C.
\]
Moreover, since \(v\) is adjacent to every vertex of \(L\),
\[
A\cong M_v\ \bar\otimes\ B,
\qquad
E_B|_A=\tau_v\otimes \operatorname{id}_B.
\]

Let
\[
K_A=L^2(A)\ominus L^2(B),
\qquad
K_C=L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B).
\]
The amalgamated free-product Hilbert space decomposition is
\[
L^2(M)
=
L^2(B)\oplus
\bigoplus_{\epsilon_1\neq\cdots\neq \epsilon_n}
K_{\epsilon_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B K_{\epsilon_n},
\]
where each \(\epsilon_i\in\{A,C\}\).

Let \(R_A\) be the projection onto \(L^2(B)\) plus all reduced AFP sectors whose first letter is \(A\). Define \(R_C\) similarly. Set
\[
P_A=R_A-e_B,\qquad P_C=R_C-e_B.
\]
Then
\[
1=e_B+P_A+P_C.
\]

We first check that
\[
R_A,R_C,P_A,P_C\in S_L(M).
\]

Let \(D\in\{A,C\}\), and let \(\overline D\) denote the other algebra. If \(d\in D^\circ:=\ker(E_B|_D)\), then right multiplication by \(d\) changes only the last AFP letter and therefore preserves both \(R_DH\) and its orthogonal complement. Hence
\[
[R_D,\rho(d)]=0.
\]
Elements of \(B\) also preserve every AFP sector, so \(R_D\) commutes with \(\rho(B)\).

If \(d\in \overline D^\circ\), then
\[
R_D=1-R_{\overline D}+e_B,
\]
and since \(R_{\overline D}\) commutes with \(\rho(d)\),
\[
[R_D,\rho(d)]=[e_B,\rho(d)].
\]
Because \(e_B\in X_L\) and \(X_L\) is invariant under left and right \(M\)-multipliers, we have
\[
[e_B,\rho(d)]\in K_L.
\]
Thus \([R_D,\rho(x)]\in K_L\) for every \(x\) in the algebraic \(*\)-algebra generated by \(A\) and \(C\).

Now let \(x\in M\). By Kaplansky density, choose uniformly bounded algebraic \(x_n\to x\) in \(\|\cdot\|_2\). For the DKEP \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-seminorm,
\[
\|\rho(x_n)-\rho(x)\|_{\infty,1}
\le \|x_n-x\|_2,
\]
because for \(\|m\|_\infty\le 1\),
\[
\|(\rho(x_n)-\rho(x))\widehat m\|_2
=
\|\widehat{m(x_n-x)}\|_2
\le \|x_n-x\|_2.
\]
Therefore
\[
\|[R_D,\rho(x_n)]-[R_D,\rho(x)]\|_{\infty,1}
\le 2\|x_n-x\|_2\to 0.
\]
Since \(K_L\) is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closed, \([R_D,\rho(x)]\in K_L\). Hence \(R_A,R_C\in S_L(M)\), and since \(e_B\in S_L(M)\), also \(P_A,P_C\in S_L(M)\).

Choose a trace-zero unitary \(u_v\in M_v\), and set
\[
a=u_v\otimes 1_B\in A.
\]
Then \(a\in A^\circ\) and \(a\) is unitary.

We next choose unitaries \(c_1,c_2\in C^\circ\) satisfying
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]

If \(|N(v)|\ge 2\), choose distinct \(w_1,w_2\in N(v)\), and let
\[
c_i=u_{w_i}\in M_{w_i}.
\]
Since \(w_i\notin L\), \(E_B(c_i)=0\). Also \(E_B(c_i^*c_i)=1\), and for \(i\neq j\), the word \(c_i^*c_j\) contains vertices outside \(L\), so \(E_B(c_i^*c_j)=0\).

If \(N(v)=\{w\}\), choose \(z\in L\) not adjacent to \(w\). Let \(u_w\in M_w\) and \(u_z\in M_z\) be trace-zero unitaries, and define
\[
c_1=u_w,\qquad c_2=u_z u_w.
\]
Then \(c_1,c_2\in C\) are unitaries. Since \(u_w\) is supported outside \(L\),
\[
E_B(c_1)=0,\qquad E_B(c_2)=u_zE_B(u_w)=0.
\]
Moreover,
\[
c_1^*c_2=u_w^*u_z u_w.
\]
Because \(z\) is not adjacent to \(w\), this is a reduced graph word containing a vertex outside \(L\), so
\[
E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0.
\]
Taking adjoints gives \(E_B(c_2^*c_1)=0\). Thus in all cases,
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]

If \(d\in C^\circ\), then left multiplication by \(d\) maps \(R_AH\) into \(P_CH\): it sends \(L^2(B)\) into \(K_C\), and it sends any sector beginning with an \(A\)-letter to a reduced sector beginning with a \(C\)-letter. Therefore
\[
c_iR_Ac_i^*\le P_C.
\]
The ranges \(c_1R_AH\) and \(c_2R_AH\) are orthogonal. Indeed, for \(\xi,\eta\in R_AH\),
\[
\langle c_1\xi,c_2\eta\rangle
=
\langle \xi,c_1^*c_2\eta\rangle.
\]
Since \(c_1^*c_2\in C^\circ\), the vector \(c_1^*c_2\eta\) belongs to \(P_CH\), which is orthogonal to \(R_AH\). Hence
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*\le P_C.
\tag{3.1}
\]

Similarly, because \(a\in A^\circ\), left multiplication by \(a\) maps \(R_CH\) into \(P_AH\), so
\[
aR_Ca^*\le P_A.
\tag{3.2}
\]

Suppose now that \(\phi\) is an \(M\)-central state on \(S_L(M)\). Since \(c_1,c_2,a\in M\) are unitaries, centrality gives
\[
\phi(c_iR_Ac_i^*)=\phi(R_A),
\qquad
\phi(aR_Ca^*)=\phi(R_C).
\]
Applying \(\phi\) to (3.1) and (3.2), we get
\[
2\phi(R_A)\le \phi(P_C),
\tag{3.3}
\]
\[
\phi(R_C)\le \phi(P_A).
\tag{3.4}
\]

Set
\[
\alpha=\phi(e_B),\qquad \beta=\phi(P_A),\qquad \gamma=\phi(P_C).
\]
Since \(e_B,P_A,P_C\) are orthogonal projections summing to \(1\),
\[
\alpha,\beta,\gamma\ge 0,\qquad \alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]
Also
\[
\phi(R_A)=\alpha+\beta,
\qquad
\phi(R_C)=\alpha+\gamma.
\]
Thus (3.3) and (3.4) become
\[
2(\alpha+\beta)\le \gamma,
\qquad
\alpha+\gamma\le \beta.
\]
Combining,
\[
\beta\ge \alpha+\gamma\ge \alpha+2(\alpha+\beta)=3\alpha+2\beta.
\]
Hence \(0\ge 3\alpha+\beta\), so \(\alpha=\beta=0\). Then \(\alpha+\gamma\le \beta\) forces \(\gamma=0\), contradicting \(\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1\).

Therefore, for every \(v\in V\), there is no \(M\)-central state on
\[
S_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}(M).
\tag{3.5}
\]

---

## 4. \(M\) has no amenable direct summand

Because \(V\) is finite and each \(M_v\) is separable, the graph product \(M\) is separable.

We show that \(M\) has no nonzero amenable direct summand. Suppose otherwise. Then there is a nonzero central projection \(z\in Z(M)\) such that \(zM\) is amenable. Equip \(zM\) with the normalized trace
\[
\tau_z(zx)=\frac{\tau(zx)}{\tau(z)}.
\]
Since \(zM\) is finite amenable, Connes’s injectivity theorem gives a conditional expectation
\[
\mathcal E:B(L^2(zM))\to zM.
\]
Then
\[
\psi=\tau_z\circ \mathcal E
\]
is a \(zM\)-central state on \(B(L^2(zM))\).

Fix \(v\in V\), and put \(L=\operatorname{Link}(v)\). Let \(p=\lambda(z)\), the projection from \(L^2(M)\) onto \(L^2(zM)\). For \(T\in S_L(M)\), define
\[
\Phi(T)=\psi(pTp|_{pH}).
\]
Then \(\Phi\) is a state on \(S_L(M)\). For \(x\in M\),
\[
p\lambda(x)p=\lambda(zx)|_{pH}\in zM.
\]
Since \(\psi\) is \(zM\)-central,
\[
\Phi(\lambda(x)T)
=
\psi((p\lambda(x)p)(pTp))
=
\psi((pTp)(p\lambda(x)p))
=
\Phi(T\lambda(x)).
\]
Thus \(\Phi\) is an \(M\)-central state on \(S_L(M)\), contradicting (3.5). Hence \(M\) has no amenable direct summand.

---

## 5. A compact-corner obstruction

We shall use the following standard consequence of the normal-bidual formulation.

### Lemma 5.1

Let \(N\) be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra with no amenable direct summand. If \(N\) is not properly proximal, then there exists an \(N\)-central state \(\Omega\) on \(\widetilde S(N)\), normal on \(N\), such that
\[
\Omega(q_K)=0,
\]
where \(q_K\) is the normal-bidual support projection of the absolute compact ideal \(K(L^2N)\).

### Proof

If \(N\) is not properly proximal, the normal-bidual criterion gives an \(N\)-central state \(\Omega\) on \(\widetilde S(N)\), normal on \(N\).

Assume \(\Omega(q_K)>0\). The compact corner \(q_K\mathcal A q_K\) is canonically the normal-bidual copy of \(B(L^2N)\). Define, for \(T\in B(L^2N)\),
\[
\Psi(T)=\frac{\Omega(q_K\,\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(T)\,q_K)}{\Omega(q_K)}.
\]
Since \(q_K\) commutes with \(N\), \(\Psi\) is an \(N\)-central state on \(B(L^2N)\).

For \(x\in N_+\),
\[
0\le q_Kxq_K\le x.
\]
Hence \(\Psi|_N\) is dominated by a scalar multiple of the normal functional \(\Omega|_N\). Therefore \(\Psi|_N\) is normal. Let
\[
\varphi=\Psi|_N.
\]
Then \(\varphi\) is a normal tracial state on \(N\). Let \(p\in Z(N)\) be the support projection of \(\varphi\). Then \(p\neq0\), and \(\varphi\) is faithful on \(pN\). Compressing \(\Psi\) to \(B(L^2(pN))\) gives a \(pN\)-central hypertrace extending the faithful normal trace \(\varphi|_{pN}\). By Connes’s hypertrace criterion, \(pN\) is amenable. This contradicts the assumption that \(N\) has no amenable direct summand.

Therefore \(\Omega(q_K)=0\). ∎

Applying Lemma 5.1 to \(M\), if \(M\) were not properly proximal, then there would exist an \(M\)-central state \(\Omega\) on \(\widetilde S(M)\), normal on \(M\), satisfying
\[
\Omega(q_K)=0.
\tag{5.1}
\]

We will derive a contradiction.

---

## 6. The support-sandwich estimate

We prove that for all \(U,W\subset V\),
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}.
\tag{6.1}
\]

Let
\[
I=U\cap W.
\]

### 6.1. Finite-propagation lemma

Let \(\mathcal A\subset M\) be the algebraic graph-product \(*\)-algebra spanned by \(1\) and reduced Wick words. For \(S\subset V\), set
\[
\mathcal A_S=\mathcal A\cap M_S.
\]
Let
\[
\mathcal C=\operatorname{span}\{\lambda(a)\rho(b):a,b\in \mathcal A\},
\qquad
\mathcal C_S=\operatorname{span}\{\lambda(a)\rho(b):a,b\in \mathcal A_S\}.
\]

We use the following graph-product normal-form fact. For full subgraphs \(S,T\subset V\), every algebraic word admits a finite \(M_T\)-\(M_S\) relative decomposition. If \(x,\widetilde x\) are reduced relative to \((T,S)\), meaning that no nontrivial initial block can be shuffled into \(M_T\) and no nontrivial terminal block can be shuffled into \(M_S\), then for every \(y\in \mathcal A_T\),
\[
E_S(\widetilde x^{\,*} y x)=0
\]
unless the relative words represented by \(x\) and \(\widetilde x\) are shuffle-equivalent. In the equivalent case, the outside letters pair off, giving the scalar \(\tau(\widetilde x^{\,*}x)\), and only those letters of \(y\) supported on vertices commuting past the middle relative word can survive the expectation. Thus
\[
E_S(\widetilde x^{\,*} y x)
=
\tau(\widetilde x^{\,*}x)\,E_Y(y)
\tag{6.2}
\]
for some \(Y\subset S\cap T\).

This is the usual graph-product Wick-word expectation formula: after shuffling commuting letters, every nonzero contribution to \(E_S\) must have all letters outside \(S\) canceled in matching pairs, and the surviving part of \(y\) must lie in \(M_{S\cap T}\) and commute through the relative word.

From (6.2) we get the finite-propagation inclusion
\[
e_S\mathcal C e_T
\subset
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C_S e_Y\mathcal C_T:Y\subset S\cap T\}.
\tag{6.3}
\]
Indeed, by linearity it suffices to consider \(\lambda(a)\rho(b)\) with \(a,b\in\mathcal A\) single relative words. Write
\[
a=a_S\widetilde x^{\,*}a_T,
\qquad
b=b_Txb_S,
\]
where
\[
a_S,b_S\in \mathcal A_S,\qquad a_T,b_T\in\mathcal A_T,
\]
and \(x,\widetilde x\) are reduced relative to \((T,S)\). For \(z\in \mathcal A_T\),
\[
e_S\lambda(a)\rho(b)e_T\widehat z
=
\widehat{E_S(azb)}.
\]
Using (6.2),
\[
E_S(azb)
=
a_S\,E_S\!\left(\widetilde x^{\,*}(a_Tzb_T)x\right)b_S
\]
is either \(0\), or else equals
\[
\tau(\widetilde x^{\,*}x)\,
a_S E_Y(a_Tzb_T)b_S
\]
for some \(Y\subset S\cap T\). Hence
\[
e_S\lambda(a)\rho(b)e_T
=
\tau(\widetilde x^{\,*}x)\,
\lambda(a_S)\rho(b_S)e_Y\lambda(a_T)\rho(b_T),
\]
which lies in \(\mathcal C_Se_Y\mathcal C_T\). This proves (6.3).

### 6.2. Algebraic compact cores and approximate units

For \(S\subset V\), let \(\mathscr T_S\) be the set
\[
\mathscr T_S
=
\{\lambda(a)\rho(b)e_S,\ e_S\rho(b^*)\lambda(a^*),\ e_S:
a,b\in\mathcal A\}.
\]
Let \(D_S\) be the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra generated by \(\mathscr T_S\).

The algebraic core \(D_S\) is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense in \(K_S\). Indeed, if \(x,y\in M\), choose uniformly bounded \(a_n,b_n\in\mathcal A\) with
\[
a_n\to x,\qquad b_n\to y
\]
in \(\|\cdot\|_2\). Then for \(\|m\|_\infty\le1\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\|(\lambda(x)\rho(y)e_S-\lambda(a_n)\rho(b_n)e_S)\widehat m\|_2\\
&\qquad =
\|xE_S(m)y-a_nE_S(m)b_n\|_2\\
&\qquad \le
\|x-a_n\|_2\|y\|_\infty
+
\|a_n\|_\infty\|y-b_n\|_2.
\end{aligned}
\]
The same estimate applies to adjoints. Thus the algebraic hereditary core is dense in the DKEP compact ideal.

For a finite subset \(F\subset \mathscr T_S\), set
\[
A_F=\sum_{s\in F}ss^*,
\]
and for \(\varepsilon>0\),
\[
h^S_{F,\varepsilon}=A_F(A_F+\varepsilon1)^{-1}.
\]
The net \((h^S_{F,\varepsilon})\), ordered by inclusion of \(F\) and by \(\varepsilon\downarrow0\), is a positive contractive approximate unit for \(D_S\). Indeed, if \(s\in F\), then
\[
\|(1-h^S_{F,\varepsilon})s\|^2
\le
\|(1-h^S_{F,\varepsilon})^2A_F\|\to0,
\]
and since \(\mathscr T_S\) is self-adjoint, the same holds on the right.

Because \(D_S\) is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense in \(K_S\), this approximate unit supports the full compact corner \(K_S\). Namely, if \(k\in K_S\) and \(\delta>0\), choose \(d\in D_S\) with
\[
\|k-d\|_{\infty,1}<\delta.
\]
Multiplication by contractions is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-contractive, hence
\[
\|(1-h_i)k\|_{\infty,1}
\le
\|k-d\|_{\infty,1}
+
\|(1-h_i)d\|_{\infty,1},
\]
and the final term tends to \(0\). Similarly \(k(1-h_i)\to0\) in \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\). Therefore the images of \(h_i\) converge weak-* to \(q_S\) in the normal bidual.

### 6.3. Triple products land in the intersection corner

For \(S\subset V\), define
\[
\mathcal P_S
=
\operatorname{span}\{C_0e_SC_1e_S\cdots e_SC_n:
n\ge1,\ C_i\in\mathcal C\}.
\]
Each \(h^S_{F,\varepsilon}\) belongs to the norm closure of \(\mathcal P_S\). Indeed, every \(ss^*\) with \(s\in\mathscr T_S\) belongs to \(\mathcal P_S\), and functional calculus by polynomials with zero constant term preserves the norm closure of \(\mathcal P_S\).

Let
\[
\mathcal Z_I
=
\operatorname{span}\{C_1e_YTe_ZC_2:
C_1,C_2\in\mathcal C,\ Y,Z\subset I,\ T\in B(H)\}.
\]
Since \(Y,Z\subset I\), we have \(e_Y,e_Z\le e_I\). Hence
\[
e_YTe_Z\in e_IB(H)e_I\subset X_I.
\]
Because \(X_I\) is invariant under the left and right \(M\)-actions, it follows that
\[
\mathcal Z_I\subset X_I.
\tag{6.4}
\]

Now suppose \(S\in\{U,W\}\). Since \(I\subset S\), the finite-propagation inclusion (6.3) gives, for \(Y\subset I\),
\[
e_S\mathcal C e_Y
\subset
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C e_Z\mathcal C:Z\subset Y\},
\]
and similarly
\[
e_Y\mathcal C e_S
\subset
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C e_Z\mathcal C:Z\subset Y\}.
\]
Thus multiplying an element of \(\mathcal Z_I\) on either side by an element of \(\mathcal P_S\) keeps all intermediate Jones projections supported on subsets of \(I\). Therefore
\[
\mathcal P_S\mathcal Z_I\subset \mathcal Z_I,
\qquad
\mathcal Z_I\mathcal P_S\subset \mathcal Z_I.
\tag{6.5}
\]

Also, for \(C_1,C_2\in\mathcal C\) and \(T\in B(H)\),
\[
e_UC_1e_W\,T\,e_WC_2e_U\in\mathcal Z_I.
\tag{6.6}
\]
Indeed, by (6.3),
\[
e_UC_1e_W
\in
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C e_Y\mathcal C:Y\subset I\},
\]
and similarly
\[
e_WC_2e_U
\in
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C e_Z\mathcal C:Z\subset I\}.
\]
Multiplying gives (6.6).

Now take
\[
P\in\mathcal P_U,\qquad Q\in\mathcal P_W,\qquad R\in\mathcal P_U.
\]
In a monomial term of \(PQR\), isolate the last \(e_U\) in \(P\), the first and last \(e_W\) in \(Q\), and the first \(e_U\) in \(R\). The middle part is of the form covered by (6.6), hence belongs to \(\mathcal Z_I\). The remaining prefix and suffix preserve \(\mathcal Z_I\) by (6.5). Therefore
\[
\mathcal P_U\mathcal P_W\mathcal P_U\subset \mathcal Z_I\subset X_I.
\tag{6.7}
\]

Let
\[
A=C^*(\lambda(M),\rho(M),e_U,e_W,e_I)\subset B(H),
\]
and let \(B_S=A\cap X_S\) for \(S\in\{U,W,I\}\). Let \(p_S\in A^{**}\) be the ordinary open support projection of \(B_S\). From (6.7), after norm closure,
\[
B_U B_W B_U\subset B_I.
\tag{6.8}
\]

Let \((h_\lambda^U)\) and \((h_\mu^W)\) be positive contractive approximate units for \(B_U\) and \(B_W\). Then
\[
h_\lambda^U h_\mu^W h_\lambda^U\in B_I^+,
\qquad
0\le h_\lambda^U h_\mu^W h_\lambda^U\le p_I
\]
inside \(A^{**}\). Letting \(\mu\to\infty\) weak-* gives
\[
0\le h_\lambda^U p_W h_\lambda^U\le p_I.
\]
Since \(0\le h_\lambda^U\le p_U\) and \(h_\lambda^U\to p_U\) weak-*, we have strong convergence \(h_\lambda^U\to p_U\). Therefore
\[
h_\lambda^U p_W h_\lambda^U\to p_U p_W p_U
\]
weak-*, and the order interval \([0,p_I]\) is weak-* closed. Hence
\[
p_U p_W p_U\le p_I.
\tag{6.9}
\]

Passing to the normal bidual, \(q_S=p_{\mathrm{nor}}p_S\), and \(p_{\mathrm{nor}}\) commutes with the \(p_S\)’s. Therefore
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U
=
p_{\mathrm{nor}}p_U p_W p_U
\le
p_{\mathrm{nor}}p_I
=
q_I.
\]
Thus
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}.
\]
This proves (6.1).

---

## 7. Relative obstruction forces full mass on link supports

Assume, toward contradiction, that \(M\) is not properly proximal. By Lemma 5.1, choose an \(M\)-central state \(\Omega\) on \(\widetilde S(M)\), normal on \(M\), such that
\[
\Omega(q_K)=0.
\tag{7.1}
\]

Let \(U\subset V\). Suppose there is no \(M\)-central state on \(S_U(M)\). We claim that
\[
\Omega(q_U)=1.
\tag{7.2}
\]

Let \(r=1-q_U\). Suppose \(\Omega(r)>0\). For \(T\in \widetilde S_U(M)\), define
\[
\Phi(T)=\frac{\Omega(rTr)}{\Omega(r)}.
\]
This is well-defined as follows. For \(x\in M\),
\[
[rTr,\rho(x)]
=
r[T,\rho(x)]r.
\]
Since \([T,\rho(x)]\) lies in the \(q_U\)-supported compact corner, we have
\[
r[T,\rho(x)]r=0.
\]
Hence \(rTr\in \widetilde S(M)\), so \(\Omega(rTr)\) is defined.

The state \(\Phi\) is \(M\)-central because \(r\) commutes with \(M\) and \(\Omega\) is \(M\)-central. Also, for \(x\in M_+\),
\[
0\le rxr\le x,
\]
so
\[
0\le \Phi(x)\le \frac{\Omega(x)}{\Omega(r)}.
\]
Since \(\Omega|_M\) is normal, \(\Phi|_M\) is normal.

Restricting \(\Phi\) to the ordinary operator system \(S_U(M)\) gives an \(M\)-central state on \(S_U(M)\), contradicting the assumption. Thus \(\Omega(r)=0\), and therefore \(\Omega(q_U)=1\).

By (3.5), this applies to \(U=\operatorname{Link}(v)\) for every \(v\in V\). Hence
\[
\Omega(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1
\qquad
\text{for every }v\in V.
\tag{7.3}
\]

---

## 8. Finite intersection and conclusion

We now combine (7.3) with the support-sandwich estimate.

Let \(\phi\) be a state extension of \(\Omega\) to the ambient normal-bidual \(C^*\)-algebra containing all the projections \(q_S\). Such an extension exists by the Hahn–Banach state-extension theorem.

Suppose \(\phi(q_U)=\phi(q_W)=1\). In the GNS representation \((\pi_\phi,H_\phi,\xi_\phi)\), we have
\[
\pi_\phi(q_U)\xi_\phi=\xi_\phi,
\qquad
\pi_\phi(q_W)\xi_\phi=\xi_\phi.
\]
Therefore
\[
\phi(q_Uq_Wq_U)=1.
\]
By (6.1),
\[
0\le q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}.
\]
Hence
\[
1=\phi(q_Uq_Wq_U)\le \phi(q_{U\cap W})\le 1,
\]
so
\[
\phi(q_{U\cap W})=1.
\]
Since \(q_{U\cap W}\in \widetilde S(M)\), this means
\[
\Omega(q_{U\cap W})=1.
\]

Iterating over the finite family
\[
U_v=\operatorname{Link}(v),\qquad v\in V,
\]
and using (7.3), we obtain
\[
\Omega\!\left(q_{\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)}\right)=1.
\tag{8.1}
\]

Because \(\Gamma\) is loopless, no vertex belongs to its own link. Therefore
\[
\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)=\varnothing.
\]
Thus (8.1) gives
\[
\Omega(q_\varnothing)=1.
\tag{8.2}
\]

Finally, \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\), so \(e_\varnothing\) is the rank-one projection onto \(\mathbb C\widehat1\subset L^2(M)\). The hereditary \(C^*\)-algebra generated by the operators \(\lambda(a)\rho(b)e_\varnothing\) is exactly \(K(L^2(M))\). Indeed,
\[
\lambda(a)e_\varnothing\lambda(b)^*
=
\theta_{\widehat a,\widehat b},
\qquad a,b\in M,
\]
and \(M\widehat1\) is dense in \(L^2(M)\). Hence
\[
X_\varnothing=K(L^2(M)),
\]
so
\[
q_\varnothing=q_K.
\tag{8.3}
\]

Combining (8.2) and (8.3), we get
\[
\Omega(q_K)=1,
\]
contradicting (7.1).

Therefore \(M\) is properly proximal. ∎

original_problem:
Prove the following statement:

Let $\Gamma$ be a finite simplicial graph with $|\VV(\GG)|\geq 3$, and $(M_v,\varphi_v)$ be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra for each $v\in \VV$. If $\Gamma$ is irreducible (in the sense that there does not exists subgraphs $\GG_1,\GG_2$ such that $ \VV(\GG_1)\cup \VV(\GG_2)=\VV(\GG) $ and every $(v,u)\in \EE(\GG) $ for all $v\in\VV(\GG_1),u\in \VV(\GG_2)$), and each $M_v$ has a trace zero unitary, then the graph product von Neumann algebra $M$ is properly proximal.
claim_verified: As written, the proof establishes the claimed theorem conditional on the DKEP/DE bidual machinery and the parabolic support sandwich lemma. After adding the routine reference, density, and finite-propagation patches above, it proves the full statement: for a finite loopless graph \(\Gamma\) with \(|V|\ge 3\), irreducible in the nontrivial-join sense, and separable tracial vertex algebras each containing a trace-zero unitary, the tracial graph product von Neumann algebra is properly proximal.
solution_verified:
## Claim

Let \(\Gamma=(V,E)\) be a finite loopless simplicial graph with \(|V|\ge 3\). Assume that \(\Gamma\) is **irreducible in the nontrivial join sense**: there is no partition
\[
V=V_1\sqcup V_2,\qquad V_1,V_2\ne\varnothing,
\]
such that every vertex of \(V_1\) is adjacent to every vertex of \(V_2\). For each \(v\in V\), let \((M_v,\tau_v)\) be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra containing a unitary \(u_v\) with \(\tau_v(u_v)=0\). Let
\[
(M,\tau)=\star_\Gamma (M_v,\tau_v)
\]
be the tracial graph product von Neumann algebra. Then \(M\) is properly proximal in the sense of Ding–Kunnawalkam Elayavalli–Peterson.

We use the standard graph-product normal form, parabolic conditional expectations, and the Caspers–Fima unscrewing decomposition
\[
M \cong M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)} *_{M_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}} M_{V\setminus\{v\}},
\]
for each \(v\in V\). These are standard facts about graph-product von Neumann algebras. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1411.2799)) We also use the DKEP/DE formulation of boundary pieces, the operator systems \(S_X(M)\), and the normal-bidual characterization of relative proper proximality. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

---

### 1. Notation and boundary pieces

Put \(H=L^2(M)\), let \(J\) be the trace modular conjugation, and write
\[
\rho(x)=Jx^*J
\]
for right multiplication by \(x\in M\). For \(U\subset V\), let \(M_U\subset M\) be the parabolic graph-product subalgebra generated by \(\{M_u:u\in U\}\), with \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\). Let
\[
E_U:M\to M_U
\]
be the trace-preserving conditional expectation, and let \(e_U:H\to L^2(M_U)\) be the Jones projection.

For \(U\subset V\), denote by \(X_U\subset B(H)\) the DKEP boundary piece associated with \(M_U\): it is the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra of \(B(H)\) generated by
\[
\{xJyJe_U:x,y\in M\}.
\]
Equivalently, \(X_U\) is the norm closure of the span of operators
\[
x_1Jy_1J\,T\,Jy_2Jx_2,\qquad x_i,y_i\in M,\quad T\in e_UB(H)e_U.
\]
In particular \(e_U\in X_U\), and \(M\) and \(JMJ\) act by multipliers of \(X_U\).

For a boundary piece \(X\), write
\[
S_X(M)=\{T\in B(H):[T,JxJ]\in K_X^{\infty,1}(M)\text{ for all }x\in M\}.
\]
DKEP relative proper proximality says that \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X\) iff there is no \(M\)-central state on \(S_X(M)\) whose restriction to \(M\) is normal. For \(X=K(H)\), we write \(S(M)\), and proper proximality means proper proximality relative to \(K(H)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

We shall also use the normal-bidual version. Let
\[
\mathfrak B=(B(H)^\sharp_J)^*
\]
be the DKEP normal bidual, and for a boundary piece \(X\), let \(q_X\) be the identity projection of the normal-bidual compact corner
\[
(K_X(M)^\sharp_J)^*\subset \mathfrak B.
\]
Then \(q_X\) commutes with both \(M\) and \(JMJ\), and
\[
\widetilde S_X(M)
=
\{T\in \mathfrak B:[T,a]\in (K_X(M)^\sharp_J)^*\text{ for all }a\in JMJ\}.
\]
DE Lemma 3.5 gives the equivalence:
\[
M\text{ is properly proximal relative to }X
\iff
\text{there is no \(M\)-central state on }\widetilde S_X(M)\text{ normal on }M.
\]
Moreover,
\[
q_X^\perp \widetilde S_X(M)q_X^\perp \subset q_K^\perp \widetilde S(M),
\]
where \(q_K\) denotes the support projection for the absolute compact boundary \(K(H)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

Because \(V\) is finite and every \(M_v\) is separable, the reduced-word span built from countable \(L^2\)-dense subsets of the \(M_v\)’s is countable and \(L^2\)-dense in \(M\). Thus \(M\) is separable, so the DKEP/DE results apply.

---

### 2. A parabolic support sandwich

We need the following support inequality.

#### Lemma 2.1 — Parabolic support sandwich

For all \(U,W\subset V\),
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}.
\]

#### Proof

Let \(\mathcal A\subset M\) be the algebraic graph-product \(*\)-algebra spanned by reduced words, and set
\[
\mathcal C=\operatorname{span}\{L_a\rho(b):a,b\in\mathcal A\}\subset B(H).
\]
For \(S\subset V\), define
\[
\mathcal P_S
=
\operatorname{span}\{A_0e_SA_1e_S\cdots e_SA_n:n\ge1,\ A_i\in\mathcal C\}.
\]

The key finite-propagation estimate is
\[
e_S\mathcal C e_T
\subset
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C e_J\mathcal C:J\subset S\cap T\}.
\tag{2.1}
\]
Indeed, for \(a,b\in\mathcal A\), the operator \(e_SL_a\rho(b)e_T\) sends \(\hat x\), \(x\in\mathcal A_T\), to
\[
\widehat{E_S(axb)}.
\]
Using the graph-product reduced-word normal form, a reduced summand of \(axb\) survives \(E_S\) only if all letters remaining after reduction lie in \(S\). Since the input letters of \(x\) lie in \(T\), every input letter that passes through the reduction and survives must lie in \(S\cap T\). The letters of \(x\) that do not pass through are precisely those cancelled or absorbed against the finitely many letters of \(a\) or \(b\). Thus, for fixed \(a,b\), the map decomposes into a finite sum of terms: left multiplication by an algebraic word, then projection onto \(L^2(M_J)\) for some \(J\subset S\cap T\), then right multiplication by an algebraic word. This gives (2.1).

Let \(I=U\cap W\). Define
\[
\mathcal Z_I
=
\operatorname{span}\{Ae_JTe_KB:
A,B\in\mathcal C,\ J,K\subset I,\ T\in B(H)\}.
\]
Since \(e_J,e_K\le e_I\), every element of \(\mathcal Z_I\) belongs to \(X_I\).

Using (2.1), multiplication on either side by an element of \(\mathcal P_U\) or \(\mathcal P_W\) preserves \(\mathcal Z_I\). Also, for \(A,B\in\mathcal C\),
\[
e_UAe_W\,T\,e_WBe_U\in\mathcal Z_I,
\]
because (2.1) gives
\[
e_UAe_W\in \operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C e_J\mathcal C:J\subset I\},
\]
and similarly for \(e_WBe_U\). Hence
\[
\mathcal P_U\mathcal P_W\mathcal P_U\subset X_I.
\tag{2.2}
\]

Now let \(Y_S\subset X_S\) be the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra generated by
\[
\{Ae_S:A\in\mathcal C\}.
\]
The algebraic core \(Y_S\) is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense in the DKEP compact piece associated with \(X_S\): the algebraic graph-product algebra \(\mathcal A\) is \(L^2\)-dense in \(M\), and for bounded \(x_i\to x\), \(y_i\to y\) in \(L^2\),
\[
x_iJy_iJe_S\to xJyJe_S
\]
in \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,2}\), hence in \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\). Hereditary products then give the density for \(Y_S\).

Choose a positive contractive approximate unit \((h_\lambda^S)_\lambda\) for \(Y_S\) of the standard form
\[
h^S_{F,\varepsilon}
=
A_{F,S}(A_{F,S}+\varepsilon)^{-1},
\qquad
A_{F,S}=\sum_{s\in F}ss^*,
\]
where \(F\) ranges over finite subsets of \(\{Ae_S:A\in\mathcal C\}\). Then
\[
h_\lambda^S\in \overline{\mathcal P_S}^{\|\cdot\|}.
\]
By DE Lemmas 3.7 and 3.8, the normal-bidual images
\[
a_\lambda^S:=\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h_\lambda^S)
\]
converge ultraweakly to \(q_S\). The required commutation with \(p_{\mathrm{nor}}\) follows because \(p_{\mathrm{nor}}\) commutes with \(M\), \(JMJ\), and \(\iota(e_S)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

Since \(h_\lambda^U\in\overline{\mathcal P_U}\) and \(h_\mu^W\in\overline{\mathcal P_W}\), (2.2) implies
\[
h_\lambda^Uh_\mu^Wh_\lambda^U\in X_I.
\]
Moreover \(p_{\mathrm{nor}}\) commutes with \(C^*(M,JMJ,e_U,e_W)\), so \(\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}\) is multiplicative on this \(C^*\)-algebra. Hence
\[
\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h_\lambda^Uh_\mu^Wh_\lambda^U)
=
a_\lambda^U a_\mu^W a_\lambda^U.
\]
The left-hand side belongs to the \(q_I\)-corner and is a positive contraction, so
\[
0\le a_\lambda^U a_\mu^W a_\lambda^U\le q_I.
\]
Letting \(\mu\to\infty\) ultraweakly gives
\[
0\le a_\lambda^U q_W a_\lambda^U\le q_I.
\]
Finally \(0\le a_\lambda^U\le q_U\) and \(a_\lambda^U\to q_U\) ultraweakly, hence strongly. Therefore
\[
a_\lambda^U q_W a_\lambda^U\to q_Uq_Wq_U
\]
ultraweakly. Since the order interval \([0,q_I]\) is ultraweakly closed,
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_I=q_{U\cap W}.
\]
This proves the lemma. \(\square\)

---

### 3. Proper proximality relative to every link boundary

Fix \(v\in V\). Write
\[
L=\operatorname{Link}(v),\qquad
B=M_L,\qquad
A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)},\qquad
C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}}.
\]
Because \(v\) is adjacent to every vertex of \(L\),
\[
A\cong M_v\bar\otimes B,
\]
and \(E_B|_A=\tau_v\otimes\operatorname{id}_B\). The graph-product unscrewing decomposition gives
\[
M=A*_B C.
\]

#### 3.1. Graph combinatorics

Since \(\Gamma\) is irreducible, \(\operatorname{Star}(v)\ne V\). Otherwise
\[
V=\{v\}\sqcup (V\setminus\{v\})
\]
would be a nontrivial join. Thus
\[
N(v):=V\setminus\operatorname{Star}(v)\ne\varnothing.
\]

If \(N(v)=\{w\}\), then \(L\ne\varnothing\), because \(|V|\ge3\). Moreover, there exists \(z\in L\) not adjacent to \(w\). Indeed, if \(w\) were adjacent to every \(z\in L\), then
\[
V=\{v,w\}\sqcup L
\]
would be a nontrivial join, contradiction.

#### 3.2. Ping-pong unitaries

Choose a trace-zero unitary \(a=u_v\in M_v\subset A\). Then
\[
E_B(a)=0.
\]

We now construct unitaries \(c_1,c_2\in C\) satisfying
\[
E_B(c_i)=0,\qquad E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\tag{3.1}
\]

If \(|N(v)|\ge2\), choose distinct \(w_1,w_2\in N(v)\), and take trace-zero unitaries \(u_{w_i}\in M_{w_i}\). Set
\[
c_i=u_{w_i}.
\]
Then \(c_i\in C\), \(E_B(c_i)=0\), and for \(i\ne j\), the reduced word \(u_{w_i}^*u_{w_j}\) contains vertices outside \(L\), so \(E_B(c_i^*c_j)=0\).

If \(N(v)=\{w\}\), choose \(z\in L\) not adjacent to \(w\). Let \(u_w\in M_w\) and \(u_z\in M_z\) be trace-zero unitaries, and define
\[
c_1=u_w,\qquad c_2=u_z u_w.
\]
Then \(c_1,c_2\in C\) are unitaries. Since \(u_z\in B\) and \(E_B(u_w)=0\),
\[
E_B(c_1)=E_B(c_2)=0.
\]
Also
\[
c_1^*c_2=u_w^*u_z u_w.
\]
Because \(z\) and \(w\) are not adjacent, the word \(w,z,w\) is graph-reduced and contains \(w\notin L\), so
\[
E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0.
\]
Similarly \(E_B(c_2^*c_1)=0\), and \(E_B(c_i^*c_i)=1\). Thus (3.1) holds.

#### 3.3. First-letter projections lie in \(S_{X_L}(M)\)

For the amalgamated free product \(M=A*_B C\), let
\[
K_A=L^2(A)\ominus L^2(B),\qquad K_C=L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B).
\]
Then
\[
L^2(M)
=
L^2(B)
\oplus
\bigoplus_{\epsilon_1\ne\cdots\ne\epsilon_n}
K_{\epsilon_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B K_{\epsilon_n},
\]
where \(\epsilon_i\in\{A,C\}\).

Let \(R_A\) be the projection onto \(L^2(B)\) plus all reduced AFP summands whose first letter is \(A\). Define \(R_C\) similarly. Put
\[
P_A=R_A-e_B,\qquad P_C=R_C-e_B.
\]
Then
\[
1=e_B+P_A+P_C.
\]

We claim
\[
R_A,R_C,P_A,P_C\in S_{X_L}(M).
\]

Let \(D\in\{A,C\}\), and write \(D^\circ=\ker E_B|_D\). If \(x\in D^\circ\), right multiplication by \(x\) can only affect the last AFP letter. Therefore it preserves the subspace \(R_DH\) and its orthogonal complement, so
\[
[R_D,\rho(x)]=0.
\]
Since
\[
R_{\bar D}=1-R_D+e_B,
\]
where \(\bar D\) denotes the other algebra,
\[
[R_{\bar D},\rho(x)]=[e_B,\rho(x)].
\]
But \(e_B=e_L\in X_L\), and \(JMJ\) acts by multipliers of \(X_L\). Hence
\[
[e_B,\rho(x)]\in K_{X_L}^{\infty,1}(M).
\]
Right multiplication by elements of \(B\) preserves every AFP sector. Since \(A\) and \(C\) generate \(M\), the DKEP generator criterion for \(S_X(M)\) gives
\[
R_A,R_C\in S_{X_L}(M).
\]
Because \(e_B\in X_L\subset S_{X_L}(M)\), also
\[
P_A,P_C\in S_{X_L}(M).
\]

#### 3.4. Projection inequalities

Let
\[
H_A^+=R_AH,\qquad H_C^+=R_CH.
\]

If \(d\in C\) is a unitary with \(E_B(d)=0\), then left multiplication by \(d\) sends \(H_A^+\) into \(P_CH\): it sends \(L^2(B)\) into \(L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B)\), and it sends a reduced word beginning with \(A\) to a reduced word beginning with \(C\). Hence
\[
dR_Ad^*\le P_C.
\]
Applying this to \(c_1,c_2\),
\[
c_iR_Ac_i^*\le P_C.
\]

Moreover \(c_1^*c_2\in C^\circ\), so \(c_1^*c_2\) sends \(H_A^+\) into \(P_CH\), which is orthogonal to \(H_A^+\). Therefore
\[
c_1H_A^+\perp c_2H_A^+,
\]
and hence
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*\le P_C.
\tag{3.2}
\]

Similarly, \(a\in A^\circ\), so left multiplication by \(a\) sends \(H_C^+\) into \(P_AH\). Therefore
\[
aR_Ca^*\le P_A.
\tag{3.3}
\]

#### 3.5. No central state on \(S_{X_L}(M)\)

Let \(\phi\) be an \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_L}(M)\). Since \(a,c_1,c_2\in M\) are unitaries,
\[
\phi(uTu^*)=\phi(T),\qquad u\in\{a,c_1,c_2\}.
\]
From (3.2),
\[
2\phi(R_A)
=
\phi(c_1R_Ac_1^*)+\phi(c_2R_Ac_2^*)
\le \phi(P_C).
\tag{3.4}
\]
From (3.3),
\[
\phi(R_C)=\phi(aR_Ca^*)\le \phi(P_A).
\tag{3.5}
\]

Set
\[
\alpha=\phi(e_B),\qquad \beta=\phi(P_A),\qquad \gamma=\phi(P_C).
\]
Then \(\alpha,\beta,\gamma\ge0\) and
\[
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]
Also
\[
\phi(R_A)=\alpha+\beta,\qquad \phi(R_C)=\alpha+\gamma.
\]
Thus (3.4) and (3.5) give
\[
2(\alpha+\beta)\le \gamma,\qquad \alpha+\gamma\le \beta.
\]
Combining,
\[
\beta\ge \alpha+\gamma\ge \alpha+2(\alpha+\beta)=3\alpha+2\beta,
\]
so
\[
0\ge 3\alpha+\beta.
\]
Hence \(\alpha=\beta=0\). Then \(\alpha+\gamma\le\beta\) forces \(\gamma=0\), contradicting
\[
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]

Therefore \(S_{X_L}(M)\) admits no \(M\)-central state. In particular, there is no \(M\)-central state normal on \(M\). Hence
\[
M\text{ is properly proximal relative to }X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}.
\tag{3.6}
\]
Since \(v\in V\) was arbitrary, (3.6) holds for every \(v\in V\).

---

### 4. \(M\) has no amenable direct summand

Assume toward contradiction that \(0\ne z\in Z(M)\) and \(zM\) is amenable. Let
\[
H_z=zH=L^2(zM)
\]
with normalized trace
\[
\tau_z(x)=\tau(z)^{-1}\tau(x),\qquad x\in zM.
\]
By the Connes injectivity–amenability theorem, amenability of \(zM\) gives a \(zM\)-central hypertrace
\[
\psi:B(H_z)\to\mathbb C
\]
extending \(\tau_z\).

Fix \(v\in V\) and put \(L=\operatorname{Link}(v)\). For \(T\in S_{X_L}(M)\), define
\[
\Phi(T)=\psi(zTz|_{H_z}).
\]
Then \(\Phi\) is a state on \(S_{X_L}(M)\). For \(x\in M\),
\[
\Phi(xT)=\psi((zx)(zTz))=\psi((zTz)(zx))=\Phi(Tx),
\]
so \(\Phi\) is \(M\)-central. Also
\[
\Phi(x)=\tau_z(zx),
\]
so \(\Phi|_M\) is normal.

This contradicts the conclusion of Section 3 that \(S_{X_L}(M)\) admits no \(M\)-central state. Hence \(M\) has no amenable direct summand.

---

### 5. From link-relative proper proximality to absolute proper proximality

Assume, for contradiction, that \(M\) is not properly proximal. Since \(M\) has no amenable direct summand, the normal-bidual characterization gives an \(M\)-central state
\[
\Omega:\widetilde S(M)\to\mathbb C
\]
normal on \(M\).

We first arrange that
\[
\Omega(q_K)=0.
\tag{5.1}
\]
Indeed, if \(\Omega(q_K)>0\), then
\[
\Psi(T)=\frac{\Omega(q_K\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(T)q_K)}{\Omega(q_K)},\qquad T\in B(H),
\]
is an \(M\)-central state on \(B(H)\). Its restriction to \(M\) is a nonzero normal tracial functional. The central support of this normal trace gives a nonzero central projection \(z\in Z(M)\), and compressing \(\Psi\) to \(B(zH)\) yields a \(zM\)-central hypertrace extending a faithful normal trace on \(zM\). Hence \(zM\) is amenable, contradicting Section 4. Therefore (5.1) holds.

Now fix \(v\in V\). By Section 3, \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\). Let
\[
q_v:=q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}.
\]
We claim
\[
\Omega(q_v)=1.
\tag{5.2}
\]
Let \(r=1-q_v\). If \(\Omega(r)>0\), define for \(T\in \widetilde S_{X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}}(M)\)
\[
\Phi(T)=\frac{\Omega(rTr)}{\Omega(r)}.
\]
The compression inclusion
\[
r\,\widetilde S_{X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}}(M)\,r
\subset
q_K^\perp \widetilde S(M)
\]
makes \(\Phi\) well-defined. Since \(r\) commutes with \(M\), \(\Phi\) is \(M\)-central. For \(x\in M_+\),
\[
0\le rxr\le x,
\]
so \(\Phi|_M\) is dominated by a normal positive functional and is therefore normal. This contradicts proper proximality relative to \(X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\). Hence \(\Omega(r)=0\), proving (5.2).

Thus
\[
\Omega(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1,\qquad v\in V.
\tag{5.3}
\]

Extend \(\Omega\) to a state \(\overline\Omega\) on the ambient \(C^*\)-algebra generated by \(\widetilde S(M)\) and all \(q_U\)’s. We use the support sandwich. Suppose \(\varphi\) is any state and \(\varphi(q_U)=\varphi(q_W)=1\). In the GNS representation of \(\varphi\), the cyclic vector is fixed by both \(q_U\) and \(q_W\). Hence
\[
\varphi(q_Uq_Wq_U)=1.
\]
By Lemma 2.1,
\[
0\le q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W},
\]
so
\[
\varphi(q_{U\cap W})=1.
\tag{5.4}
\]

Applying (5.4) inductively to the finite family
\[
\{\operatorname{Link}(v):v\in V\},
\]
and using (5.3), we obtain
\[
\overline\Omega\!\left(q_{\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)}\right)=1.
\]
Because \(\Gamma\) is loopless, no vertex belongs to its own link. Therefore
\[
\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)=\varnothing.
\]
Thus
\[
\overline\Omega(q_\varnothing)=1.
\tag{5.5}
\]

Finally, \(X_\varnothing=K(H)\). Indeed, \(e_\varnothing\) is the rank-one projection onto \(\mathbb C\hat 1\), and for \(a,b\in M\),
\[
L_ae_\varnothing L_b^*=\theta_{\hat a,\hat b}.
\]
Since \(M\hat 1\) is dense in \(H\), these rank-one operators span a norm-dense subalgebra of \(K(H)\). Conversely, the boundary piece generated by \(e_\varnothing\) consists of compact operators. Hence
\[
X_\varnothing=K(H),
\]
and therefore
\[
q_\varnothing=q_K.
\tag{5.6}
\]

Combining (5.1), (5.5), and (5.6),
\[
1=\overline\Omega(q_\varnothing)=\overline\Omega(q_K)=\Omega(q_K)=0,
\]
a contradiction.

Therefore \(M\) is properly proximal. \(\square\)

1. **Finite-propagation patch for Lemma 2.1.**  
For \(R\subset V\), write \(L^2(M_R)\) as the sum of reduced-word sectors whose vertex words use only vertices in \(R\). It suffices to prove the estimate for \(a,b\) single algebraic reduced words. For \(x\in\mathcal A_T\),
\[
e_S L_a\rho(b)e_T\hat x=\widehat{E_S(axb)}.
\]
The reduction of \(axb\) involves only finitely many cancellations/absorptions between the fixed words \(a,b\) and the prefix/suffix of \(x\). Any letter of \(x\) that survives into \(E_S(axb)\) must have vertex in \(S\), while every letter of \(x\) already has vertex in \(T\); hence the surviving middle word lies in \(M_J\) for some \(J\subset S\cap T\). The finitely many canceled prefix/suffix letters contribute scalar coefficient functionals of the form \(m\mapsto\tau_v(c_1mc_2)\), implemented by algebraic left/right multipliers and \(e_\varnothing\). Thus one obtains a finite formula
\[
E_S(axb)=\sum_r \alpha_r\,E_{J_r}(\beta_r x\gamma_r)\,\delta_r,
\qquad J_r\subset S\cap T,
\]
with \(\alpha_r,\beta_r,\gamma_r,\delta_r\in\mathcal A\). Equivalently,
\[
e_S L_a\rho(b)e_T
=\sum_r L_{\alpha_r}\rho(\delta_r)\,e_{J_r}\,L_{\beta_r}\rho(\gamma_r)
\in \operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C e_J\mathcal C:J\subset S\cap T\}.
\]
Linearity gives (2.1). The same formula also proves that multiplication by \(\mathcal P_U\) or \(\mathcal P_W\) preserves \(\mathcal Z_I\), yielding \(\mathcal P_U\mathcal P_W\mathcal P_U\subset X_{U\cap W}\).

2. **Density and approximate-unit patch in Lemma 2.1.**  
Let
\[
\mathcal G_S=\{L_a\rho(b)e_S:a,b\in\mathcal A\}.
\]
The hereditary algebra generated by \(\mathcal G_S\) is
\[
Y_S=\overline{\operatorname{span}}\{rTr'{}^*:r,r'\in\mathcal G_S,\ T\in B(H)\}.
\]
For \(x,y\in M\), choose uniformly bounded \(a_n,b_n\in\mathcal A\) with \(a_n\to x\), \(b_n\to y\) in \(L^2\). Then
\[
\|(L_{a_n}\rho(b_n)-L_x\rho(y))e_S\|_{\infty,2}\to0
\]
because \(E_S\) is \(L^\infty\)-contractive and \(L^2\)-contractive. Hence the algebraic generators \(L_a\rho(b)e_S\) are \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,2}\), therefore \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\), dense among the generators \(xJyJe_S\) of \(X_S\). Applying the same estimate on both sides of \(rTr'{}^*\) gives \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-density of \(Y_S\) in \(K_{X_S}^{\infty,1}(M)\).  
For finite \(F\subset\mathcal G_S\),
\[
A_{F,S}=\sum_{s\in F}ss^*\in\mathcal P_S,
\]
and since \(t\mapsto t(t+\varepsilon)^{-1}\) vanishes at \(0\), \(h^S_{F,\varepsilon}=A_{F,S}(A_{F,S}+\varepsilon)^{-1}\in\overline{\mathcal P_S}\). DE Lemma 3.8 gives \(p_{\rm nor}\iota(e_S)=\iota(e_S)p_{\rm nor}\), and \(p_{\rm nor}\) also commutes with \(M\) and \(JMJ\); hence \(p_{\rm nor}\) commutes with all \(h^S_{F,\varepsilon}\). DE Lemma 3.7 then gives \(\iota_{\rm nor}(h^S_{F,\varepsilon})\to q_S\) ultraweakly.

3. **First-letter projection patch.**  
For \(T=R_A\) or \(R_C\), define
\[
M_0(T)=\{x\in M:[T,JxJ],[T,Jx^*J]\in K_{X_L}^{\infty,1}(M)\}.
\]
By DKEP Lemma 6.1, \(M_0(T)\) is a von Neumann subalgebra. For \(x\in D^\circ=\ker E_B|_D\), right multiplication by \(x\) only changes the last AFP letter, so it preserves the first-letter sectors for \(R_D\); hence \([R_D,\rho(x)]=0\). For the opposite first-letter projection,
\[
R_{\bar D}=1-R_D+e_B,
\]
so \([R_{\bar D},\rho(x)]=[e_B,\rho(x)]\in X_L\subset K_{X_L}^{\infty,1}(M)\). Elements of \(B\) preserve all AFP sectors. Thus \(A,C\subset M_0(T)\), and since \(A,C\) generate \(M\), \(R_A,R_C\in S_{X_L}(M)\).

4. **Patch for \(\Omega(q_K)>0\Rightarrow\) amenable summand.**  
If \(\Omega(q_K)>0\), define
\[
\Psi(T)=\frac{\Omega(q_K\iota_{\rm nor}(T)q_K)}{\Omega(q_K)},\qquad T\in B(H).
\]
Because \(q_K\) commutes with \(M\), \(\Psi\) is \(M\)-central. For \(x\in M_+\),
\[
0\le q_Kxq_K\le x,
\]
so \(\Psi|_M\) is dominated by the normal functional \(\Omega|_M\), hence is normal. Centrality makes \(\Psi|_M\) tracial. Let \(z\in Z(M)\) be the support of this nonzero normal trace. Then
\[
\Psi_z(T)=\frac{\Psi(zTz)}{\Psi(z)},\qquad T\in B(zH),
\]
is a \(zM\)-central hypertrace extending a faithful normal tracial state on \(zM\). By the Connes injectivity/amenability characterization, \(zM\) is amenable, contradicting Section 4. Hence \(\Omega(q_K)=0\).

5. **Patch for the state-extension/GNS sandwich step.**  
Since each \(q_U\) commutes with \(JMJ\), it belongs to the ambient absolute \(\widetilde S(M)\)-operator system. A state on an operator system extends to any containing unital \(C^*\)-algebra by the standard state-extension theorem. If a state \(\varphi\) on that \(C^*\)-algebra satisfies \(\varphi(q_U)=\varphi(q_W)=1\), then in the GNS representation the cyclic vector is fixed by both projections, so \(\varphi(q_Uq_Wq_U)=1\). Lemma 2.1 then gives \(1\le \varphi(q_{U\cap W})\le1\), proving \(\varphi(q_{U\cap W})=1\).
</PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>

original_problem:
Prove the following statement:

Let $\Gamma$ be a finite simplicial graph with $|\VV(\GG)|\geq 3$, and $(M_v,\varphi_v)$ be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra for each $v\in \VV$. If $\Gamma$ is irreducible (in the sense that there does not exists subgraphs $\GG_1,\GG_2$ such that $ \VV(\GG_1)\cup \VV(\GG_2)=\VV(\GG) $ and every $(v,u)\in \EE(\GG) $ for all $v\in\VV(\GG_1),u\in \VV(\GG_2)$), and each $M_v$ has a trace zero unitary, then the graph product von Neumann algebra $M$ is properly proximal.

solution_verified:
## Original Problem

Prove the following statement:

Let $\Gamma$ be a finite simplicial graph with $|\VV(\GG)|\geq 3$, and $(M_v,\varphi_v)$ be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra for each $v\in \VV$. If $\Gamma$ is irreducible (in the sense that there does not exists subgraphs $\GG_1,\GG_2$ such that $ \VV(\GG_1)\cup \VV(\GG_2)=\VV(\GG) $ and every $(v,u)\in \EE(\GG) $ for all $v\in\VV(\GG_1),u\in \VV(\GG_2)$), and each $M_v$ has a trace zero unitary, then the graph product von Neumann algebra $M$ is properly proximal.

## Problem Solved

We prove the following self-contained version of the stated theorem.

Let \(\Gamma=(V,E)\) be a finite simple loopless graph with \(|V|\ge 3\). We interpret “irreducible” in the standard graph-product sense intended in the problem statement: there is no partition
\[
V=V_1\sqcup V_2,\qquad V_1,V_2\ne\varnothing,
\]
such that every vertex of \(V_1\) is adjacent to every vertex of \(V_2\). For each \(v\in V\), let \((M_v,\tau_v)\) be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra containing a unitary \(u_v\in M_v\) with \(\tau_v(u_v)=0\). Let
\[
(M,\tau)=\ast_\Gamma (M_v,\tau_v)
\]
be the tracial graph-product von Neumann algebra. Then \(M\) is properly proximal in the DKEP/DE sense: equivalently, the absolute normal-bidual boundary operator system of \(M\) admits no \(M\)-central state whose restriction to the canonical left copy of \(M\) is normal.

This is the original theorem, with “irreducible” read as the standard no-nontrivial-join-partition condition.

## Proof

### 1. Graph-product notation and standard facts

For \(U\subset V\), let \(M_U\subset M\) be the graph-product von Neumann subalgebra generated by \(\{M_u:u\in U\}\), with the convention
\[
M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1.
\]
Let
\[
E_U:M\to M_U
\]
be the trace-preserving conditional expectation, and let
\[
e_U:L^2(M)\to L^2(M_U)
\]
be the Jones projection.

For a vertex \(v\in V\), write
\[
\operatorname{Link}(v)=\{w\in V\setminus\{v\}: w\sim v\},
\qquad
\operatorname{Star}(v)=\operatorname{Link}(v)\cup\{v\}.
\]

We use the standard graph-product reduced-word facts.

1. Reduced graph words span a weakly dense \(*\)-subalgebra of \(M\). If a reduced graph word contains at least one vertex outside \(U\), then \(E_U\) kills it.

2. For each \(v\in V\), with \(L=\operatorname{Link}(v)\),
\[
M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)}
\cong
M_v\ \bar\otimes\ M_L
\]
trace-preservingly, and under this identification
\[
E_L|_{M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)}}=\tau_v\otimes \operatorname{id}_{M_L}.
\]

3. For each \(v\in V\), there is a trace-preserving amalgamated free product decomposition
\[
M
=
M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)}
\ast_{M_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}}
M_{V\setminus\{v\}}.
\]

4. We also use the following relative reduced-word expectation principle. If \(S,T\subset V\), then for algebraic graph-product words \(a,b\), the operator
\[
e_S L_aR_b e_T
\]
factors through finitely many Jones projections \(e_J\) with \(J\subset S\cap T\). More explicitly,
\[
e_S\,\mathcal C\,e_T
\subset
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C_S e_J\mathcal C_T:J\subset S\cap T\},
\tag{1}
\]
where \(\mathcal C\) is the algebraic span of left-right multipliers \(L_aR_b\), \(a,b\) algebraic graph-product words, and \(\mathcal C_S\) is the analogous span with \(a,b\in M_S\). This follows from the usual relative normal form and the conditional expectation formula for graph products.

Since \(V\) is finite and the \(M_v\)’s are separable, \(M\) is separable.

---

### 2. DKEP/DE boundary framework used in the proof

Let
\[
H=L^2(M),\qquad J=J_M
\]
be the modular conjugation, and write
\[
\rho(x)=Jx^*J,\qquad x\in M,
\]
for the right action of \(M\) on \(H\).

For each \(U\subset V\), let \(X_U\) be the DKEP boundary piece associated with the subalgebra \(M_U\). Concretely, \(X_U\) is the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra generated by the left-right \(M\)-translates of \(e_U\), equivalently by large corners coming from \(e_U B(H)e_U\).

Let \(K^{\infty,1}_{X_U}(M)\) denote the corresponding DKEP \(X_U\)-compact ideal. It is a closed self-adjoint \(M\)-\(M\) and \(JMJ\)-\(JMJ\) bimodule containing \(X_U\). Define the concrete relative operator system
\[
S_{X_U}(M)
=
\{T\in B(H):[T,\rho(x)]\in K^{\infty,1}_{X_U}(M)\ \text{for all }x\in M\}.
\]
A state \(\omega\) on \(S_{X_U}(M)\) is \(M\)-central if
\[
\omega(xT)=\omega(Tx)
\qquad
(x\in M,\ T\in S_{X_U}(M)).
\]
The inclusion \(M\subset M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_U\) if there is no \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_U}(M)\) whose restriction to the left copy of \(M\) is normal.

We also use the normal-bidual formulation. There is an ambient normal-bidual von Neumann algebra \(\mathcal N\), containing the left copy of \(M\), \(JMJ\), and support projections \(q_U\) for the boundary pieces \(X_U\). The projection \(q_U\) is the identity/support projection of the normal-bidual compact corner associated with \(X_U\). The compact support projection is denoted by \(q_K\), corresponding to \(K(H)\).

We use the following standard DKEP/DE facts.

- Concrete relative proper proximality is equivalent to the corresponding normal-bidual formulation.

- The support projections \(q_U\) commute with \(M\) and \(JMJ\).

- If \(M\) has no amenable direct summand and is not properly proximal, then there exists an \(M\)-central state \(\Omega\) on the absolute normal-bidual boundary operator system, normal on \(M\), such that
\[
\Omega(q_K)=0.
\tag{2}
\]

- If \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_U\), then every obstruction state \(\Omega\) as in (2) satisfies
\[
\Omega(q_U)=1.
\tag{3}
\]
Indeed, if \(r=1-q_U\) and \(\Omega(r)>0\), then the DKEP/DE compression inclusion
\[
r\,\widetilde S_{X_U}(M)\,r
\subset
\widetilde S(M)
\]
allows one to define
\[
\Phi(T)=\frac{\Omega(rTr)}{\Omega(r)},
\qquad T\in \widetilde S_{X_U}(M).
\]
Because \(r\) commutes with \(M\), \(\Phi\) is \(M\)-central. For \(x\in M_+\),
\[
0\le r x r\le x,
\]
so \(\Phi|_M\) is dominated by a normal functional and is therefore normal. This contradicts relative proper proximality to \(X_U\). Hence \(\Omega(q_U)=1\).

---

### 3. Fixed-vertex link-relative ping-pong

Fix \(v\in V\). Put
\[
L=\operatorname{Link}(v),\qquad
B=M_L,\qquad
A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)},\qquad
C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}}.
\]
By the graph-product AFP decomposition,
\[
M=A\ast_B C.
\]
Let
\[
A^\circ=\ker(E_B|_A),\qquad
C^\circ=\ker(E_B|_C).
\]
The \(L^2\)-space decomposes as
\[
L^2(M)
=
L^2(B)
\oplus
\bigoplus_{\substack{n\ge1\\ \epsilon_i\in\{A,C\}\\ \epsilon_i\ne \epsilon_{i+1}}}
K_{\epsilon_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B K_{\epsilon_n},
\]
where
\[
K_A=L^2(A)\ominus L^2(B),
\qquad
K_C=L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B).
\]

Let \(P_A\) be the projection onto the reduced AFP summands whose first letter is \(A\), and let \(P_C\) be the projection onto the reduced AFP summands whose first letter is \(C\). Then
\[
1=e_B+P_A+P_C.
\]
Set
\[
R_A=e_B+P_A,\qquad
R_C=e_B+P_C.
\]

#### 3.1. The first-letter projections lie in \(S_{X_L}(M)\)

Let \(D\in\{A,C\}\). Right multiplication by an element of \(D\) preserves the subspace
\[
R_D L^2(M)=L^2(B)\oplus P_D L^2(M)
\]
and its orthogonal complement. Indeed, right multiplication only changes the final AFP letter; the first AFP letter is unchanged except that a one-letter \(D\)-word may collapse into \(L^2(B)\), which is still contained in \(R_D L^2(M)\). Right multiplication by \(B\) preserves every AFP sector. Therefore
\[
[R_D,\rho(x)]=0
\qquad
(x\in D).
\tag{4}
\]

Since
\[
R_A+R_C=1+e_B,
\]
we get, for \(x\in D\),
\[
[R_{\overline D},\rho(x)]
=
[e_B,\rho(x)],
\]
where \(\overline D\) is the other algebra. Because \(e_B\in X_L\) and the \(X_L\)-compact ideal is stable under \(JMJ\)-multipliers,
\[
[e_B,\rho(x)]\in K^{\infty,1}_{X_L}(M).
\]
Hence, for \(T=R_A\) or \(R_C\),
\[
[T,\rho(x)]\in K^{\infty,1}_{X_L}(M)
\qquad
(x\in A\cup C).
\]

The algebra generated by \(A\cup C\) is weakly dense in \(M\). Using the commutator identity
\[
[T,\rho(xy)]
=
[T,\rho(y)]\rho(x)+\rho(y)[T,\rho(x)]
\]
and the \(JMJ\)-bimodule property of \(K^{\infty,1}_{X_L}(M)\), the same conclusion holds for the algebraic \(*\)-algebra generated by \(A\cup C\). Finally, if \(z_i\to z\) in \(\|\cdot\|_2\) with \(\sup_i\|z_i\|_\infty<\infty\), then
\[
\|[T,\rho(z_i-z)]\|_{\infty,1}
\le
2\|T\|\,\|z_i-z\|_2,
\]
so closedness of \(K^{\infty,1}_{X_L}(M)\) gives
\[
[T,\rho(z)]\in K^{\infty,1}_{X_L}(M)
\qquad
(z\in M).
\]
Thus
\[
R_A,R_C\in S_{X_L}(M).
\]
Since \(e_B\in S_{X_L}(M)\), also
\[
P_A=R_A-e_B,\qquad P_C=R_C-e_B
\]
belong to \(S_{X_L}(M)\).

#### 3.2. Choosing the ping-pong unitaries

Because
\[
A\cong M_v\bar\otimes B,
\]
choose a trace-zero unitary \(u_v\in M_v\) and put
\[
a=u_v\otimes 1_B\in A.
\]
Then \(a\) is unitary and
\[
E_B(a)=0,\qquad E_B(a^*a)=1.
\tag{5}
\]

Let
\[
N(v)=V\setminus\operatorname{Star}(v)
\]
be the set of non-neighbors of \(v\). Irreducibility implies \(N(v)\ne\varnothing\): if \(N(v)=\varnothing\), then \(v\) is adjacent to every vertex in \(V\setminus\{v\}\), so
\[
V=\{v\}\sqcup(V\setminus\{v\})
\]
would be a nontrivial join partition.

We claim that there exist unitaries \(c_1,c_2\in C\) such that
\[
E_B(c_i)=0,\qquad
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\tag{6}
\]

If \(|N(v)|\ge2\), choose distinct \(w_1,w_2\in N(v)\), and set
\[
c_i=u_{w_i}\in M_{w_i}\subset C.
\]
Each \(c_i\) is a reduced word containing a vertex outside \(L\), so \(E_B(c_i)=0\). For \(i\ne j\), the product \(c_i^*c_j\) is still a reduced graph word containing vertices outside \(L\), hence \(E_B(c_i^*c_j)=0\), while \(E_B(c_i^*c_i)=1\).

It remains to treat the case \(N(v)=\{w\}\). Since \(|V|\ge3\), the link \(L\) is nonempty. If \(w\) were adjacent to every vertex of \(L\), then
\[
V=\{v,w\}\sqcup L
\]
would be a nontrivial join partition. Hence there exists \(z\in L\) with \(z\not\sim w\). Choose trace-zero unitaries \(u_w\in M_w\) and \(u_z\in M_z\), and set
\[
c_1=u_w,\qquad
c_2=u_z u_w.
\]
Then \(c_1,c_2\in C\) are unitaries. The words \(u_w\) and \(u_z u_w\) both contain the vertex \(w\notin L\), so
\[
E_B(c_1)=E_B(c_2)=0.
\]
Moreover,
\[
c_1^*c_2=u_w^*u_z u_w
\]
is reduced because \(z\not\sim w\). It contains \(w\notin L\), hence
\[
E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0.
\]
Similarly \(E_B(c_2^*c_1)=0\), and \(E_B(c_i^*c_i)=1\). Thus (6) holds.

#### 3.3. Projection inequalities

Let
\[
H_A^+=R_A L^2(M),
\qquad
H_C^+=R_C L^2(M).
\]
If \(d\in C^\circ\), then left multiplication by \(d\) sends \(H_A^+\) into \(P_C L^2(M)\): it sends \(L^2(B)\) into \(K_C\), and it prepends a centered \(C\)-letter to any reduced word beginning with an \(A\)-letter. Hence
\[
dR_A d^*\le P_C.
\tag{7}
\]
Applying this to \(c_1,c_2\), and using \(E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0\), the subspaces \(c_1H_A^+\) and \(c_2H_A^+\) are orthogonal. Indeed, for \(\xi,\eta\in H_A^+\),
\[
\langle c_1\xi,c_2\eta\rangle
=
\langle \xi,c_1^*c_2\eta\rangle
=
0,
\]
because \(c_1^*c_2\in C^\circ\) sends \(H_A^+\) into \(P_C L^2(M)\), which is orthogonal to \(H_A^+\). Therefore
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*
\le
P_C.
\tag{8}
\]

Similarly, since \(a\in A^\circ\), left multiplication by \(a\) sends \(H_C^+\) into \(P_A L^2(M)\). Hence
\[
aR_Ca^*
\le
P_A.
\tag{9}
\]

#### 3.4. No \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_L}(M)\)

Suppose \(\omega\) is an \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_L}(M)\). Since \(S_{X_L}(M)\) is an \(M\)-bimodule, centrality implies
\[
\omega(uTu^*)=\omega(T)
\]
for every unitary \(u\in M\) and every \(T\in S_{X_L}(M)\).

Set
\[
\alpha=\omega(e_B),\qquad
\beta=\omega(P_A),\qquad
\gamma=\omega(P_C).
\]
Because \(e_B,P_A,P_C\) are orthogonal projections summing to \(1\),
\[
\alpha,\beta,\gamma\ge0,
\qquad
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\tag{10}
\]

Applying \(\omega\) to (8) gives
\[
2\omega(R_A)\le \omega(P_C),
\]
that is,
\[
2(\alpha+\beta)\le \gamma.
\tag{11}
\]
Applying \(\omega\) to (9) gives
\[
\omega(R_C)\le \omega(P_A),
\]
that is,
\[
\alpha+\gamma\le \beta.
\tag{12}
\]
Combining (11) and (12),
\[
\beta
\ge
\alpha+\gamma
\ge
\alpha+2(\alpha+\beta)
=
3\alpha+2\beta.
\]
Thus
\[
0\ge 3\alpha+\beta.
\]
Since \(\alpha,\beta\ge0\), we get \(\alpha=\beta=0\). Then (12) gives \(\gamma\le0\), so \(\gamma=0\), contradicting (10).

Therefore \(S_{X_L}(M)\) admits no \(M\)-central state at all. In particular, \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\). Since \(v\in V\) was arbitrary, this holds for every vertex \(v\).

---

### 4. \(M\) has no amenable direct summand

Assume, toward contradiction, that there is a nonzero central projection \(z\in Z(M)\) such that \(zM\) is amenable. Let \(\psi\) be a \(zM\)-central hypertrace on \(B(zL^2(M))\) extending the normalized trace on \(zM\).

Fix any vertex \(v\), and let \(X=X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\). For \(T\in S_X(M)\), define
\[
\Phi(T)=\psi(zTz|_{zL^2(M)}).
\]
Then \(\Phi\) is a state on \(S_X(M)\). For \(x\in M\),
\[
\Phi(xT)
=
\psi((zx)(zTz))
=
\psi((zTz)(zx))
=
\Phi(Tx),
\]
so \(\Phi\) is \(M\)-central. Moreover, for \(x\in M\),
\[
\Phi(x)=\frac{\tau(zx)}{\tau(z)},
\]
so \(\Phi|_M\) is normal. This contradicts the fixed-vertex ping-pong result above. Hence \(M\) has no amenable direct summand.

---

### 5. The parabolic support sandwich

We now prove that for all \(U,W\subset V\),
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}.
\tag{13}
\]
No commutativity of \(q_U,q_W\) is claimed.

Let \(\mathcal A\subset M\) be the algebraic graph-product \(*\)-algebra spanned by reduced words. For \(a,b\in\mathcal A\), write
\[
L_aR_b(\widehat x)=\widehat{axb}.
\]
Let
\[
\mathcal C=\operatorname{span}\{L_aR_b:a,b\in\mathcal A\},
\]
and, for \(S\subset V\),
\[
\mathcal C_S=\operatorname{span}\{L_aR_b:a,b\in\mathcal A\cap M_S\}.
\]

For \(S\subset V\), define
\[
\mathscr L_S=\{L_aR_b e_S:a,b\in\mathcal A\}.
\]
Let \(D_S\) be the hereditary \(C^*\)-algebra generated by \(\mathscr L_S\), equivalently the norm closure of the span of elements
\[
sTt^*,
\qquad
s,t\in\mathscr L_S,\ T\in B(H).
\]
This is an algebraic core for \(X_S\), and it is dense in the \(X_S\)-compact ideal in the DKEP \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-topology.

For finite \(F\subset \mathscr L_S\) and \(\varepsilon>0\), set
\[
A_{F,S}=\sum_{s\in F}ss^*,
\qquad
h^S_{F,\varepsilon}
=
A_{F,S}(A_{F,S}+\varepsilon)^{-1}.
\]
The net \(h^S_{F,\varepsilon}\) is a positive contractive approximate unit for \(D_S\). It belongs to
\[
C^*(M,JMJ,e_S).
\]
The normal-bidual embedding \(\iota_{\rm nor}:B(H)\to\mathcal N\) is not globally multiplicative, but it is multiplicative on each algebra
\[
C^*(M,JMJ,e_U,e_W),
\tag{14}
\]
because the normal support projection used in the compression defining \(\iota_{\rm nor}\) commutes with the universal images of \(M\), \(JMJ\), \(e_U\), and \(e_W\).

The support-limit property gives
\[
\iota_{\rm nor}(h^S_{F,\varepsilon})
\longrightarrow
q_S
\]
weak-* in \(\mathcal N\).

#### 5.1. Finite propagation

We prove the finite propagation inclusion
\[
e_S\mathcal C e_T
\subset
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C_S e_J\mathcal C_T:J\subset S\cap T\}.
\tag{15}
\]
It is enough to consider \(L_aR_b\) with \(a,b\in\mathcal A\). Using the relative graph-product normal form, write \(a\) as a finite sum of terms
\[
a_S a_0 a_T,
\qquad
a_S\in\mathcal A\cap M_S,\ a_T\in\mathcal A\cap M_T,
\]
where \(a_0\) is reduced relative to \((S,T)\). Similarly write \(b\) as a finite sum of terms
\[
b_T b_0 b_S,
\qquad
b_T\in\mathcal A\cap M_T,\ b_S\in\mathcal A\cap M_S,
\]
with \(b_0\) reduced relative to \((T,S)\).

For \(z\in\mathcal A\cap M_T\),
\[
E_S(azb)
=
a_S\,E_S\!\bigl(a_0(a_Tzb_T)b_0\bigr)\,b_S.
\]
The reduced-word conditional expectation formula says that this is either \(0\), or equals a scalar multiple of
\[
a_S E_J(a_Tzb_T)b_S
\]
for some \(J\subset S\cap T\). Hence, as an operator \(L^2(M_T)\to L^2(M_S)\),
\[
e_S L_aR_b e_T
\in
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C_S e_J\mathcal C_T:J\subset S\cap T\}.
\]
This proves (15).

#### 5.2. Algebraic \(U\)-\(W\)-\(U\) triples land in \(X_{U\cap W}\)

Fix \(U,W\subset V\), and put
\[
I=U\cap W.
\]
Let
\[
\mathcal P_S
=
\operatorname{span}\{C_0e_SC_1e_S\cdots e_SC_n:
n\ge1,\ C_i\in\mathcal C\}.
\]
Each \(h^S_{F,\varepsilon}\) lies in the norm closure of \(\mathcal P_S\), because \(A_{F,S}\in\mathcal P_S\), \(\mathcal P_S\) is an algebra, and the function \(t\mapsto t(t+\varepsilon)^{-1}\) is uniformly approximable on bounded intervals by polynomials with zero constant term.

Define
\[
\mathcal Z_I
=
\operatorname{span}\{A e_J T e_K B:
A,B\in\mathcal C,\ J,K\subset I,\ T\in B(H)\}.
\]
Since \(e_J,e_K\le e_I\) whenever \(J,K\subset I\), we have
\[
\mathcal Z_I\subset X_I.
\tag{16}
\]

Using (15), if \(S\in\{U,W\}\), then
\[
\mathcal P_S\mathcal Z_I\subset\mathcal Z_I,
\qquad
\mathcal Z_I\mathcal P_S\subset\mathcal Z_I.
\tag{17}
\]
Indeed, when \(J\subset I\subset S\), the propagation formula applied to \(e_SC e_J\) or \(e_JCe_S\) only produces projections \(e_L\) with \(L\subset J\subset I\).

Also, for \(A,B\in\mathcal C\) and \(T\in B(H)\),
\[
e_UAe_W\,T\,e_WBe_U\in\mathcal Z_I.
\tag{18}
\]
This follows from (15), because
\[
e_UAe_W
\in
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C_Ue_J\mathcal C_W:J\subset I\},
\]
and similarly
\[
e_WBe_U
\in
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C_We_K\mathcal C_U:K\subset I\}.
\]

Combining (17) and (18), every algebraic triple product satisfies
\[
\mathcal P_U\mathcal P_W\mathcal P_U
\subset
\mathcal Z_I
\subset
X_I.
\tag{19}
\]
Passing to norm limits gives
\[
h^U_\lambda h^W_\mu h^U_\lambda\in X_I
\tag{20}
\]
for the approximate units above.

#### 5.3. Weak-* order limit

Let
\[
a_\lambda=\iota_{\rm nor}(h^U_\lambda),
\qquad
b_\mu=\iota_{\rm nor}(h^W_\mu).
\]
Then
\[
a_\lambda\to q_U,\qquad b_\mu\to q_W
\]
weak-* in \(\mathcal N\).

Because \(h^U_\lambda,h^W_\mu\in C^*(M,JMJ,e_U,e_W)\), the restricted multiplicativity (14) gives
\[
a_\lambda b_\mu a_\lambda
=
\iota_{\rm nor}(h^U_\lambda h^W_\mu h^U_\lambda).
\]
By (20), the operator \(h^U_\lambda h^W_\mu h^U_\lambda\) is a positive contraction in the \(X_I\)-compact ideal. Hence its normal-bidual image is supported by \(q_I\), and
\[
0\le a_\lambda b_\mu a_\lambda\le q_I.
\tag{21}
\]
Letting \(\mu\to\infty\), separate weak-* continuity of multiplication in \(\mathcal N\) yields
\[
0\le a_\lambda q_W a_\lambda\le q_I.
\tag{22}
\]
Now \(0\le a_\lambda\le q_U\) and \(a_\lambda\to q_U\) weak-*. Therefore \(a_\lambda\to q_U\) strongly: for every normal positive functional \(\phi\),
\[
\phi((q_U-a_\lambda)^2)
\le
\phi(q_U-a_\lambda)
\to0.
\]
Thus
\[
a_\lambda q_W a_\lambda
\to
q_Uq_Wq_U
\]
weak-*. Since the order interval \([0,q_I]\) is weak-* closed, (22) gives
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_I=q_{U\cap W}.
\]
This proves the support sandwich (13).

We emphasize that the proof used neither global multiplicativity of \(\iota_{\rm nor}\), nor a product inclusion \(X_UX_W\subset X_{U\cap W}\), nor commutativity or equality \(q_Uq_W=q_{U\cap W}\).

---

### 6. The empty parabolic boundary is the compact boundary

For \(U=\varnothing\),
\[
M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1.
\]
Thus \(e_\varnothing\) is the rank-one projection onto \(\mathbb C\widehat 1\). For \(a,b\in M\),
\[
L_a e_\varnothing L_b^*
=
\theta_{\widehat a,\widehat b}.
\]
The vectors \(M\widehat 1\) are dense in \(L^2(M)\), so these rank-one operators span a norm-dense subalgebra of \(K(L^2(M))\). Conversely, all left-right translates of \(e_\varnothing\) are compact. Hence
\[
X_\varnothing=K(L^2(M)),
\]
and therefore
\[
q_\varnothing=q_K.
\tag{23}
\]

---

### 7. Final contradiction

Assume, toward contradiction, that \(M\) is not properly proximal.

By Section 4, \(M\) has no amenable direct summand. Therefore the DKEP/DE obstruction theorem gives an \(M\)-central state \(\Omega\) on the absolute normal-bidual boundary operator system, normal on \(M\), such that
\[
\Omega(q_K)=0.
\tag{24}
\]

For every \(v\in V\), Section 3 proves that \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\). Hence the compression fact from Section 2 gives
\[
\Omega(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1
\qquad
(v\in V).
\tag{25}
\]

To multiply support projections, extend \(\Omega\) to a state \(\overline\Omega\) on the ambient \(C^*\)-algebra generated by the normal-bidual operator system and all support projections. This extension preserves the values in (24) and (25).

We use the following elementary GNS fact. If \(\phi\) is a state on a unital \(C^*\)-algebra and \(p,s\) are projections with
\[
\phi(p)=\phi(s)=1,
\]
then
\[
\phi(psp)=1.
\]
Indeed, in the GNS representation, \(p\) and \(s\) both fix the cyclic vector.

Now take \(U,W\subset V\). If
\[
\overline\Omega(q_U)=\overline\Omega(q_W)=1,
\]
then
\[
\overline\Omega(q_Uq_Wq_U)=1.
\]
By the support sandwich,
\[
0\le q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W},
\]
so
\[
\overline\Omega(q_{U\cap W})=1.
\tag{26}
\]

Iterating (26) over the finite family
\[
U_v=\operatorname{Link}(v),\qquad v\in V,
\]
and using (25), we obtain
\[
\overline\Omega\left(q_{\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)}\right)=1.
\]
Because \(\Gamma\) is loopless, no vertex lies in its own link, so
\[
\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)=\varnothing.
\]
Hence
\[
\overline\Omega(q_\varnothing)=1.
\]
By (23),
\[
q_\varnothing=q_K,
\]
so
\[
\overline\Omega(q_K)=1.
\]
This contradicts (24).

Therefore \(M\) is properly proximal. ∎
